title.emphasis: Part V: A Novel Theory of Duty – Joint Employer Liability as a Vector for Processor Accountability

section.para: A novel but powerful legal theory further strengthens the claims against payment processors by reframing their relationship with digital creators. By applying California's expansive joint employer doctrine, it is possible to argue that processors are not merely arms-length vendors but co-employers who share direct legal duties and liabilities. This theory serves to pierce the corporate structuring of the modern "fissured" digital workplace, where essential functions are outsourced to diffuse accountability.50

title.emphasis: 5.1 California's Expansive Joint Employer Doctrine: The Martinez v. Combs Test

section.para: California law defines the employment relationship broadly. Under the seminal case Martinez v. Combs, an entity can be deemed a joint employer if it exercises control over an individual's (1) wages, (2) hours, or (3) working conditions.51 Critically, this control need not be direct; the doctrine explicitly covers entities that exercise
indirect control, preventing dominant companies from escaping liability by delegating employment functions to intermediaries.51

title.emphasis: 5.2 Processor AUPs as Indirect Control Over "Working Conditions"

section.para: This framework can be applied directly to the role of payment processors in the creator economy. For a digital creator, the "working conditions" are the rules that govern the creation, distribution, and monetization of their content. When a payment processor's AUP dictates what subject matter is permissible, what themes are prohibited, and what forms of expression are eligible for monetization, it is exercising profound and direct control over the essential terms of the creator's labor.52

This control is not a post-hoc penalty but a form of ex ante regulation that shapes the work from its inception. Creators are forced to self-censor and conform their creative output to the processor's AUP to ensure they can be paid.52 This constitutes significant indirect control over the "manner and means" of performing the work, a core element of the employment test.

title.emphasis: 5.3 The Power to Deny Payment as Control Over "Wages"

section.para: Furthermore, the payment processor holds an absolute veto over a creator's ability to receive remuneration. By unilaterally freezing funds, terminating an account, or blocking transactions, the processor exercises ultimate control over the "method of payment".52 This power to permit or deny payment is a direct form of control over "wages" as defined under California's joint employer test.

If a payment processor is deemed a joint employer alongside a platform like Patreon or Itch.io, it can no longer maintain the fiction of being a neutral third-party vendor. It would acquire a direct legal relationship with the creator, which carries with it a higher duty of care and fair dealing. Under this framework, arbitrary and discriminatory enforcement of an AUP would not be a mere breach of a commercial contract but a violation of duties owed to an employee.

This status would also expose processors to cascading liability. If creators on a platform were successfully re-characterized as misclassified employees of that platform, a processor deemed a joint employer could be held jointly and severally liable for the platform's wage theft and other labor code violations.54 This theory re-integrates the processor into the employment relationship, forcing it to bear the legal responsibilities that correspond to the immense control it already wields.
